narrative: Dear [Student's Name], imagine the admissions team looking at your application as a story of your journey toward becoming a lawyer, piecing together the chapters from your undergraduate days through law school to predict if you'd pass the bar on the first try. Your story starts strong in their eyes with your undergraduate GPA of 2.8 and LSAT score of 28.3, but what weighed heaviest was how you ranked among your peers once in law school—landing in the 2nd decile for Year 3 grades was the single biggest signal pulling toward concerns about bar success, even more than your 1st decile standing in Year 1. These early and later academic rankings painted a picture of consistent struggle to rise above the middle of the pack, which the model saw as the strongest predictor of potential failure, tipping the scales to a 53.9% estimated risk. Layered on top was your upper-middle family income, which in their data often correlates with added pressures that haven't always led to bar passage for similar students. Your undergraduate GPA and LSAT further reinforced this narrative, suggesting a foundation that might not fully support the intense bar demands ahead. Even factors like being a white male pursuing full-time studies nudged the story toward caution, as patterns in past applicants with these traits showed slightly higher hurdles. It's not that any one piece defined you—your full-time commitment shows real dedication—but together, they formed a composite portrait where the model couldn't confidently bet on passage. This is why your application was rejected; it's a tough call based on probabilities from thousands of similar paths. The good news is, this isn't the end of your story—you can strengthen those academic deciles with targeted prep or retakes, turning the page toward success. Many have rewritten their narratives just like this. Keep pushing; your determination is already a brighter chapter.
